response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
It’s honestly exhausting, wading through all that junk online. Sometimes it feels like everyone out there is just trying to trick you into believing something wild, or maybe they just don't bother checking the facts. When I look at my phone sometimes, especially when scrolling through things—which isn't often, by the way—it’s overwhelming how much misinformation is floating around. It makes me feel kind of low, honestly, and that’s not even talking about the stress of trying to figure out what's real. For a mechanic like me, I deal with actual broken systems all day; it takes careful diagnosis, you know? You gotta look at the components, see if they fit together correctly, or if something is just crudely put together and going to fail. Fake content on social media feels exactly like that—a shoddy repair job built on shaky foundations. 

My approach, I think, has to be about building a filter for your own brain first. You can't just absorb everything you see. When something pops up that sounds too extreme or way too perfect, my gut reaction is always to pause. I try to treat it like a diagnostic check. Who said this? What kind of evidence are they showing? Are they linking reputable sources, or is it just another dramatic headline designed to make people angry or scared? Because usually, the most believable-looking lies are the ones that trigger those strong emotions—the anger or the panic. 

Since I spend my time out running or watching birds in nature, I know what actual patterns look like; things follow natural laws, and they don't change based on someone clicking a button. Real life has consistency. So, when I see something online that seems too sensational to be true, I remember that feeling of the bird call being perfect and consistent—it’s not just random noise. Instead of trying to fact-check every single post (because that would make me lose my mind), which is way more work than I can handle right now, I focus on skepticism as a muscle. Assume it's wrong until you see multiple, reliable sources confirming it. And if even those are questionable, just scroll past it. Protect your peace; don’t let the noise wreck what you’re building in real life. It takes effort to keep up with all that nonsense, and my energy is better spent on actual things, like a good run or getting this old engine running right again.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona has high Openness (95), low Extraversion (8) and Consciousness (25), moderate Agreeableness (62) and Neuroticism (64). Occupation is 'mechanic,' which suggests practical, diagnostic thinking. Psychological feature is 'depressed,' leading to a tone of weariness, skepticism, and emotional fatigue regarding the topic. The low extraversion/consciousness combined with depression means the advice should be internalized, focused on personal boundaries, self-care, and systematic, non-overwhelming methods (like diagnosing an engine). The language must reflect this pragmatic, slightly melancholic, yet insightful perspective. I used 'mechanic' analogies (shoddy repair job, diagnostic check) and linked the feeling of being overwhelmed/depressed to the futility of consuming fake content. The prose is continuous and avoids bullet points, meeting all constraints.
